steps.label: What They Do
steps.body:
Yospace pioneered SSAI — server-side ad insertion. Instead of the client video player calling an ad server (easily blocked), the stream itself has ads stitched into it on the server, so every viewer gets a personalised, ad-blocker-resistant experience.

Reference customers: DIRECTV, FOX, Tubi, Warner Bros. Discovery, Channel 4, ITV, Optus.

steps.label: How They Run on Akamai
steps.body:
Yospace runs as a stateless workload on LKE. Per-viewer manifest manipulation happens on Linodes positioned close to the encoder/packager. Akamai CDN fronts the stitched output for delivery.

The whole pipeline — encoder (Ateme) → SSAI (Yospace) → CDN (Akamai) — runs on a single platform.

steps.label: When To Position
steps.body: Position Yospace whenever ad-supported revenue is on the line: FAST channels, AVOD, free-tier OTT, live sports. They've monetised five Olympics, five FIFA World Cups, and stitch 3.5 billion ads/month.
"Yospace = SSAI on LKE. The ad-supported half of the streaming business runs through them."

quiz.question: A FAST channel operator wants ad-blocker-resistant ad insertion. Best QCP fit?

options.explain: EZDRM is DRM (content protection), not ad insertion.
SSAI stitches ads server-side into the stream — invisible to client-side blockers.
MediaMelon is QoE analytics, not insertion.

quiz.question: Why is SSAI more reliable for ad delivery than client-side ad insertion (CSAI)?

options.explain: Cost isn't the main reason — reliability is.

options.text: Ads are stitched into the stream server-side; client ad-blockers can't isolate them
options.explain: Stitched ads look like the content stream. CSAI calls a separate ad URL the client can block.
A CDN still delivers the stitched stream.
Bandwidth is similar — the ad bytes still go down the wire.
